message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 3/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"At 16 syllables/second (ultra-fast condition), blind subjects' comprehension ranged from 0 to 93% (mean 59.7%, SD 24.0%), whereas sighted controls consistently fell below 16% (mean 9.0%, SD 4.7%). This quantifies a massive trained-listener vs untrained gap at ultra-fast rates and confirms ~16 syl/s as a meaningful design ceiling distinguishing the two intended use cases (general students vs trained power listeners)."

**Source:** https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "extended...from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)... performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)"

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

| Scientific American","url":"https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/why-can-some-blind-people-process/"}]

Based on the web search results, I found research by Dietrich, Hertrich, and Ackermann on ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind individuals. Here are the key findings:

## Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Study

The research indicates that in blind listeners the capability for ultra-fast speech comprehension (16 syllables per second) covaries with hemodynamic activation of right-hemisphere primary visual area (V1), left fusiform gyrus (FG) and bilateral pulvinar.

The proficiency of the blind subjects extended from low to high comprehension capabilities (up to >90%—in terms of the percentage of syllables in correctly reproduced words in a sentence repetition task) at 16 syl/s, whereas the performance level of sighted subjects fell consistently below 20%.

## Key Research Findings

Individuals suffering from vision loss of a peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second—exceeding by far the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s).

The studies were published in neuroscience journals, including BMC Neuroscience and Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, and utilized functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to investigate how the brain processes ultra-fast speech in blind individuals.

- PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3745084/"},{"title":"Network Modeling for Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) Signals during Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Late-Blind Listeners | PLOS One","url":"https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0132196"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study | BMC Neuroscience | Full Text","url":"https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/"}]

I found the research paper you're looking for! Here's what the search revealed:

The document with ID "1471-2202-14-74" is a peer-reviewed study titled "Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study" by Susanne Dietrich, Ingo Hertrich, and Hermann Ackermann, published in July 2013.

**Key Findings on Speech Comprehension Rates:**

Individuals suffering from vision loss of a peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second - exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (approximately 8 syl/s).

**Study Design:**

The research used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) performed in blind subjects of varying ultra-fast speech comprehension capabilities and sighted individuals while listening to sentence utterances of a moderately fast (8 syl/s) or ultra-fast (16 syl/s) syllabic rate.

**Brain Activation:**

Blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv).

This is a significant finding showing how blind individuals can develop extraordinary speech comprehension skills that far exceed typical listening rates, and how their visual cortex is repurposed for this task.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The PMC open-access version is available. Let me fetch the exact numbers from it to verify the specific statistics in the claim.

input.prompt: Extract exact statistics about comprehension performance at the ultra-fast (16 syllables/second) speech condition. I need EXACT quotes for: (1) Confirm the ultra-fast condition was 16 syllables per second. (2) For BLIND subjects at 16 syl/s: the comprehension range, mean, and SD — look for "0 to 93%", "mean = 59.7%", "SD ± 24.0%". (3) For SIGHTED control subjects: their performance — look for "below 16%" OR "below 20%", "mean = 9.0%", "SD ± 4.7%". (4) Critically: does the sighted controls' threshold say "below 16%" or "below 20%"? Quote the exact sentence. (5) What does this percentage measure — is it percent of syllables in correctly reproduced words in a sentence repetition task? Quote the exact sentences for each of these.

content.content:
# Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Statistics

**(1) Ultra-fast condition confirmation (16 syl/s):**
"20 out of the total of 40 sentences were compressed to a moderately fast (8 syl/s) and the remaining 20 items to an ultra-fast syllabic rate (16 syl/s"

**(2) Blind subjects' performance at 16 syl/s:**
"Comprehension of ultra-fast speech utterances (16 syl/s) – in terms of the percentage of correctly reproduced items of 10-word sentences outside the scanner – extended in blind listeners from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, _SD_ ± 24.0%)"

**(3) Sighted control subjects' performance:**
"In sighted individuals, performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, _SD_ ± 4.7%)"

**(4) Critical threshold quote (below 16%, not 20%):**
"In sighted individuals, performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, _SD_ ± 4.7%)"

**(5) What the percentage measures:**
"To obtain a quantitative behavioral measure of an individual's capability to understand moderately fast and ultra-fast speech utterances, each subject performed – outside the scanner and prior to the fMRI measurements – a sentence repetition task, encompassing a subset of the forward stimulus materials (ten sentences at both speaking rates each). These verbal utterances were truncated to the phrase-initial 9–10 words in order to limit memory load."

content.text:
I have the exact numbers from the primary open-access source. Let me verify each component of the claim against the source.

The claim states:
- "At 16 syllables/second (ultra-fast condition)" ✓ confirmed (16 syl/s)
- "blind subjects' comprehension ranged from 0 to 93% (mean 59.7%, SD 24.0%)" ✓ exact match: "extended in blind listeners from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)"
- "sighted controls consistently fell below 16% (mean 9.0%, SD 4.7%)" ✓ exact match: "performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)"

The measurement is percentage of correctly reproduced items of 10-word sentences (sentence repetition task) — a valid comprehension/intelligibility proxy.

Now let me check the interpretive part of the claim for overreach. The claim adds an inference: "confirms ~16 syl/s as a meaningful design ceiling distinguishing the two intended use cases." Let me note the methodological caveats. The sample: blind subjects ranged 0-93%, meaning some blind subjects also performed at 0%. The study is about late-blind trained listeners, n is small (fMRI study), and "16 syl/s" was the test condition, not a derived "ceiling." But the numeric claim itself is precisely supported.

Let me confirm sample sizes and whether the "trained vs untrained" framing is accurate, since the claim emphasizes a "trained-listener vs untrained gap."

input.prompt: I need details about the subjects/sample. (1) How many blind subjects and how many sighted controls participated? Give exact numbers. (2) Were the blind subjects "trained" specifically for this study, or were they everyday screen-reader/text-to-speech users who had naturally acquired this skill? Quote any text about whether blind subjects were trained or were habitual ultra-fast speech users. (3) Were blind subjects congenitally blind or late-blind? (4) Was 16 syl/s presented as the maximum/ceiling, or just one test condition? Did the paper mention rates up to 22 syl/s? Quote exact sentences.

content.content:
# Study Sample Details

## (1) Subject Numbers
- **Blind subjects:** 14 (11 males; mean age = 35.1 years)
- **Sighted controls:** 12 (9 males; mean age = 30.3 years)

## (2) Training Status
The blind subjects were **not trained for this study**—they were habitual users with naturally acquired skills. The paper states: "repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second" and notes they "considerably benefit...from screen-reading text-to-speech systems, operating at ultra-fast syllable rates."

## (3) Blindness Onset
**Mixed group:** The sample included both early-blind (n=3, from birth or very young) and late-blind subjects (n=11, vision loss occurring after age 1). The paper notes "both subjects with early- as well as late-onset blindness have been found capable...to acquire the capacity of ultra-fast speech comprehension."

## (4) Speech Rate Testing
The study presented **16 syl/s as the experimental test condition**, not a maximum. The paper mentions the broader context: "understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables...exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)." So 22 syl/s represents real-world capability, while 16 syl/s was the laboratory stimulus rate.

content.text:
I now have everything needed to assess the claim. Let me finalize my verdict.

The verbatim numbers in the claim are an exact, precise match to the primary open-access source (PMC3847124, BMC Neuroscience 2013, Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann):
- 16 syl/s ultra-fast condition ✓
- Blind: 0 to 93%, mean 59.7%, SD 24.0% ✓ (exact match)
- Sighted: below 16%, mean 9.0%, SD 4.7% ✓ (exact match — and notably the claim correctly uses "16%" not the "20%" that the search-engine summary loosely paraphrased)

The measurement is a valid intelligibility proxy (percent correctly reproduced words in a sentence-repetition task). The "trained vs untrained gap" framing is well-supported: blind subjects were habitual ultra-fast TTS/screen-reader users (naturally trained), sighted controls were not.

Minor interpretive caveats (do not refute the core claim): (a) the claim labels 16 syl/s a "design ceiling" — the paper actually frames 16 syl/s as the lab test rate and notes real-world blind capability up to ~22 syl/s, so 16 is more of a test point than a hard ceiling; (b) n=14 blind / n=12 sighted is a small fMRI sample; (c) blind range starts at 0%, meaning some blind subjects also failed — so the gap is about a *trained subpopulation*, not all blind people. But these refine, not refute. The quantitative claim is accurate and the source is a peer-reviewed primary study.

This claim is NOT refuted.

input.evidence: All quantitative figures match the primary open-access source EXACTLY (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann, BMC Neuroscience 2013, 14:74; PMC3847124). Verbatim from the paper: "Comprehension of ultra-fast speech utterances (16 syl/s)... extended in blind listeners from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)" and "In sighted individuals, performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)." The claim correctly cites "below 16%" (a search-engine summary loosely paraphrased this as "below 20%", but the actual paper says 16% — the claim got it right). The metric is a valid intelligibility proxy: percent of correctly reproduced words in a 10-word sentence-repetition task done outside the scanner. The 16 syl/s ultra-fast condition is confirmed ("20 items to an ultra-fast syllabic rate (16 syl/s)"). The trained-vs-untrained framing is supported: blind subjects (n=14) were habitual ultra-fast screen-reader/TTS users (naturally trained), sighted controls (n=12) untrained. Source is peer-reviewed primary research — matches claim strength. Caveats that REFINE but do not refute: (1) the paper frames 16 syl/s as the lab test rate, not a hard "ceiling" — it notes real-world blind capability up to ~22 syl/s, so calling 16 a "design ceiling" is a reasonable but mild over-reading; (2) small fMRI sample (n=14/12); (3) blind range starts at 0%, so the gap reflects a trained subpopulation, not all blind people. The core numeric claim is accurate, current for its purpose (foundational 2013 neuroscience, not a fast-moving benchmark), and from a primary source.
input.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found. The only discrepancy was a WebSearch AI-summary that paraphrased "below 16%" as "below 20%", but direct fetch of the primary PMC3847124 full text confirms the paper says "below 16%", matching the claim. Related papers by the same group (Frontiers fnhum.2013.00701; PLOS One pone.0132196) corroborate the ~22 syl/s real-world blind capability vs ~8 syl/s for sighted.

content.text: Verification complete. The claim is NOT refuted — its statistics match the primary source verbatim and the source quality is appropriate.
